title: Calling CopyToContainer through the podman socket does not preserve UID/GID information
body:
### Issue Description

When using the Docker SDK to call the `CopyToContainer` API through the `podman.sock`, Podman is not preserving the UID and GID of the folders and files in the TAR archive even when passing the `CopyUIDGID: true` option. Every folder and file are being copied into the container as `root:root`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Clone test code - `https://github.com/dwaynebradley/docker-copytocontainer-example.git`
   * The sample code calls `CopyToContainer` API and passes the `CopyUIDGID: true` in the `CopyToContainerOptions`
1. View contents of the tar file to see permissions that have been set on the example folder and file - `tar -tvf test-configs.tar`
   * `configs` will be owned by `1001:1001`
   * `configs/test-config-file.txt` will be owned by `1002:1002`
1. Start Podman systemd service - `systemctl --user enable --now podman.service`
1. `export DOCKER_HOST=unix://${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/podman/podman.sock`
1. Run the test code to create the container that copies the contents of `test-configs.tar` into the container - `go run main.go`


### Describe the results you received

1. Exec into the container that was created by the sample code - `podman exec -ti copy-uidgid-test /bin/sh`
1. Check ownership on the contents copied into the container
   * `stat /configs` - it will be owned by `root:root`
   * `stat /configs/test-config-file.txt` - if will also be owned by `root:root`


### Describe the results you expected

I expected both the folder and file to have the same ownership as what is stored in the `test-configs.tar` file.

If I run this exact same code using Docker instead, the ownership on both the file and folder are retained inside of the container correctly.

### podman info output

comments.body:
Here is the contents of the tar file:

```
❯ tar -tvf test-configs.tar
drwxr-xr-x 1001/1001         0 2025-10-20 22:25 configs/
-rw-r--r-- 1002/1002        36 2025-10-20 22:25 configs/test-config-file.txt
```

Here is the output of the `stat` commands I mentioned above that I am seeing after running the sample code with Podman:

```
/ # stat /configs
  File: /configs
  Size: 4096            Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   directory
Device: 7eh/126d        Inode: 351515      Links: 2
Access: (0755/drwxr-xr-x)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
Access: 2025-10-21 02:47:56.827825000 +0000
Modify: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Change: 2025-10-21 02:47:56.826763211 +0000
/ # stat /configs/test-config-file.txt
  File: /configs/test-config-file.txt
  Size: 36              Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 7eh/126d        Inode: 351516      Links: 1
Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
Access: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Modify: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Change: 2025-10-21 02:47:56.826763211 +0000
```

Here is the output of the `stat` commands that I am seeing after running the sample code with Docker:

```
/ # stat /configs
  File: /configs
  Size: 4096            Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   directory
Device: 91h/145d        Inode: 687639      Links: 2
Access: (0755/drwxr-xr-x)  Uid: ( 1001/ UNKNOWN)   Gid: ( 1001/ UNKNOWN)
Access: 2025-10-21 02:52:50.255233409 +0000
Modify: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Change: 2025-10-21 02:52:24.259191323 +0000
/ # stat /configs/test-config-file.txt
  File: /configs/test-config-file.txt
  Size: 36              Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 91h/145d        Inode: 687640      Links: 1
Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: ( 1002/ UNKNOWN)   Gid: ( 1002/ UNKNOWN)
Access: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Modify: 2025-10-21 02:25:22.000000000 +0000
Change: 2025-10-21 02:52:24.259191323 +0000
```

The logic for managing file ownership during copy operations is inverted between the Docker and Podman APIs. In Docker, `copyUIDGID=true` preserves the original UID/GID from the archive. Conversely, Podman requires `Chown=false` to achieve the same result. I am working on fix.
Thanks for the fix on this one @Honny1 😎

I wonder, have you tried also against "normal" Docker?

I tried this against `moby 28.5.1` on Fedora and it appears that is behaves the same.
It's contraintuitive  but `copyUIDGID`/`--archive` does **not** mean to preserve ownership from the tar stream. It means: apply the main uid/gid of the container. It's insane but that's how things work.

See also: https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/34096

I just ran it again using Docker on Debian. Here is that version info:

```

```

I ran the exact same test with Docker 28.5.2 on Arch Linux and it behaves the exact same way. `CopyUIDGID: true` is preserving the ownership from the tar archive. It is not applying ownership the based on the user inside of the container.

>I ran the exact same test with Docker 28.5.2 on Arch Linux and it behaves the exact same way. CopyUIDGID: true is preserving the ownership from the tar archive. It is not applying ownership the based on the user inside of the container.

@dwaynebradley 
OK this is quite surprising for me since my Docker just one patch release below behaves exact opposite.

Would you run this test on your Docker?
https://gist.github.com/matejvasek/859f161d3bb91fed171e218a545d70d7

just adjust the the docker hosts array:
https://gist.github.com/matejvasek/859f161d3bb91fed171e218a545d70d7#file-test-go-L34-L38
to contain only your socket.
See this comment: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27379#issuecomment-3529488773

I tired to run the test on `ubuntu 25.04` with `docker 29.0.0` and the result is the same for me.
`copyUIDGID: false` uses uid/gid from the tar strem
`copyUIDGID: true` uses uid/gid from the container

Btw it looks like there was a PR changing this to more logical way of working:
https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/34099
but it has not been merged
and at his point if they "fixed" it would break a lot of software relying on the current weird behaviour.

@dwaynebradley would you mind sharing your testing code in it's entirety?
I shared mine. The two of us observe exact opposite behaviour of Docker and I cannot make sense of it.

It seems that the Docker documentation is not accurate to the actual behavior.  [Docker rest API reference](https://docs.docker.com/reference/api/engine/version/v1.52/#tag/Container/operation/PutContainerArchive:~:text=copyUIDGID-,string,will%20copy%20UID/GID%20maps%20to%20the%20dest%20file%20or%20dir,-REQUEST%20BODY%20SCHEMA)

I agree that "fixing" this issue would cause a lot of problems for other software relying on it. Therefore, fixing the documentation would probably be less invasive for Docker.
https://github.com/moby/moby/discussions/51523

> @dwaynebradley would you mind sharing your testing code in it's entirety?
> I shared mine. The two of us observe exact opposite behaviour of Docker and I cannot make sense of it.

My example code is here:

https://github.com/dwaynebradley/docker-copytocontainer-example

Definitely make sure that my example code is consistent with what yours is doing.

Thank you for providing it! @dwaynebradley 

I tried to run your example and then modify [main](https://github.com/dwaynebradley/docker-copytocontainer-example/blob/master/main.go#L51) `CopyUIDGID => false`.

In both cases that the output is the same. Whether `CopyUIDGID` is true or false the ids from tar are used.
I am just confused more and more.

Your example as opposed to mine seems to use image that use root (0:0) user. My example deliberately has user 1011:1012.
So I tried changing user to non-root, in my case 42:43. With that I observe the behaviour described above.
CopyUIDGID: false == use tar ids
CopyUIDGID: true == use ctr ids

It looks like for container that runs as a root the flag do not matter at all at least on docker v29.0.0.
It always preserves ids from tar.
Also I use busybox image not alpine, not sure if that could cause the difference in our results.

@dwaynebradley  OK it looks like if the image does not have explicitly set User (like your image `alpine:latest`) the then ids from tar are always used because container ids cannot be used since they are not defined. EDIT: Even though empty User implies root:root in practise when container is spawned.
@dwaynebradley plz try using some other image in your test that has explicitly set User (no matter to what).
See `docker image inspect alpine:latest | jq '.[0].ContainerConfig.User'`

Possibly that's the actual difference between `podman` and `docker` that you observed?
Not what `copyUIDGID` does, but rather how containers with no explicit uses behave?
However such a containers sound like edge case to me, usually you would use container with an explicit user, right?
Either way, if you want preserve uid/gid from tar you are supposed to set `copyUIDGID==true` IMO.

So you are IMO right there is a bug in `podman` right now. But it's not about `copyUIDGID` inversion.

The bug should be something like:
```
docker ignores copyUIDGID for containers using images that have no explicit user and ALWAYS use tar uid/gids,
however podman in such a case assumes root user and acts accordingly (so with copyUIDGID=true the copied files are owned root).
```
Does it make sense?

Well, that is even worse behavior! 🤷🏻‍♂️😂 

I will run my test against a couple of other images but either way, the `CopyToContainer` behavior SHOULD be consistent across both Docker and Podman no matter what image we use when creating a container. Looking at the docs link that @Honny1 sent, it looks like is **_should_** be:

* `CopyUIDGID: true` SHOULD always be "copy UID/GID from the source dirs/files copied to dest"
* `CopyUIDGID: false` SHOULD always be "use UID/GID from the container for the dest dirs/files that were copied"

But apparently this is completely mixed bag of what behavior we get based on what runtime we use + what image was used to create the container. Not good at all here.

I am in a situation where I cannot guarantee what image was used for creating the container that I am copying to. Those are defined and built by other people, not me, so I really do not have any control over that.

Fun times here... 😂

> it looks like is should be:

unfortunately it looks like it's exact opposite, we will see what docker devs will respond in https://github.com/moby/moby/discussions/51523

I already had fun time when somebody tried change query param `pull` from `bool` to `string`/`enum of pull policies`.
The documentation said that the `pull` query param is a `string` so understandably they try to fix that in podman so it accepts pull policies, e.g. `PullAlaways`, `PullIfNotPresent`. Well... the doc lied, it was in fact a `bool` and the change to the `enum` break compatibility

Ever since I do not trust the docker API documentation at all.
I just read docker client/server code to know the truth.

>I am in a situation where I cannot guarantee what image was used for creating the container that I am copying to. Those are defined and built by other people, not me, so I really do not have any control over that.

Well at least you should be able to explicitly set User when calling ContainerStart.

I agree, let's wait and see what the Docker devs have to say. At the end of the day, as long as both Docker and Podman behave the same way so that we can work with either and they do the same thing consistently, that will be a win.

>I agree, let's wait and see what the Docker devs have to say. 

Almost a month latter and still no response on moby/moby#51523
